title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - Probe Detection Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting probe detection issues on the Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound system.

steps.title: Verify Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the probe is fully seated in the correct port. Check for any dirt, debris, or corrosion on the probe connector or system port. Why: Loose or dirty connections are the most common cause of detection failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Cable
steps.instructions: Look for visible damage such as cuts, kinks, or fraying. Gently straighten any bends and ensure strain relief is intact. Why: Damaged cables can interrupt communication signals to the system.

steps.title: Swap Ports (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: If the system has multiple probe ports, try connecting the probe to a different port. Why: Determines if the issue is the probe or the system port.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Probe
steps.instructions: Connect a different, verified working probe to the same port. Why: Confirms whether the issue lies with the original probe or the system electronics.

steps.title: Check System Software and Firmware
steps.instructions: Verify that the system software supports the probe type being used. Look for any recent updates or alerts from Siemens Healthineers regarding probe compatibility. Why: Software mismatches can cause detection errors even when hardware is functional.

steps.title: Restart the System
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled system shutdown and restart. Why: Resets system communication and can clear temporary faults.

steps.title: Document Observations
steps.instructions: Record probe type, port used, error codes, and any patterns noticed. Why: Documentation aids in future troubleshooting or escalation.

CCR.Complaint: No Probe Connected message on the ACUSON Sequoia system.
CCR.Cause: Probe cable or connector may be loose or damaged; system may not be recognizing the probe.
CCR.Resolution: Verified connections and cable integrity, swapped probe port, tested with known-good probe, restarted system. If problem persisted, system and/or probe labeled Out of Service and sent for service evaluation.

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - Poor Image Quality or Artifacts
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound system experiencing poor image quality, streaking, shadowing, or other artifacts.

steps.title: Confirm the Probe Selection and Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct probe is selected in the system. Check that the probe is fully seated in the connector and locked. Why: Loose connections can cause intermittent signals or artifacts.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe and Cable
steps.instructions: Visually check for cracks, bent pins, or frayed cables. Swap with a known-good probe if available to see if the issue persists. Why: Damaged probes are a frequent source of poor image quality.

steps.title: Check the Imaging Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the preset, depth, gain, and focus settings match the exam type. Reset to default imaging settings to rule out user-adjusted parameters. Why: Incorrect settings can mimic hardware artifacts.

steps.title: Verify the System Display and Monitor
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor or display connections are secure. Check brightness, contrast, and monitor calibration. Why: Display issues can appear as image artifacts.

steps.title: Power Cycle the System
steps.instructions: Turn off the system completely, wait 30 seconds, and restart. Why: Temporary software or memory glitches can cause artifacts.

steps.title: Check Environmental and External Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure the patient area is free from strong electromagnetic interference. Check for nearby devices like cautery units or mobile phones. Why: External interference can affect image acquisition.

CCR.Complaint: Images are fuzzy with intermittent streaks; probe appears connected but artifacts persist.
CCR.Cause: Observed that the image artifact persisted with multiple probes and after adjusting settings; display and cable connections verified; external interference ruled out.
CCR.Resolution: System removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Siemens Healthineers for bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Probe types tested and serial numbers
Image presets and settings used during testing

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - System Boot Failures
description: Guide for troubleshooting Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia systems that fail to boot or freeze during startup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never attempt troubleshooting on a system in active patient use. Move patients to an alternative imaging system first. Ensure safe environment before performing any electrical or software tests.

steps.title: Verify Power Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is plugged into a live, grounded outlet. Check for illuminated power indicators on the system. Why: Many boot failures are caused by loss of AC power or poor grounding.

steps.title: Check Power Cables and Surge Protection
steps.instructions: Inspect AC power cord for damage or loose connections. Bypass extension cords or surge protectors temporarily to test. Why: Faulty cables or overloaded surge protectors can prevent startup.

steps.title: Check Battery Backup (If Present)
steps.instructions: Some ACUSON Sequoia systems have internal batteries; verify charge status. Replace or remove battery and attempt boot with AC power only. Why: A failing internal battery can halt boot processes.

steps.title: Confirm Peripheral Devices
steps.instructions: Disconnect all non-essential peripherals (external drives, USB devices, printers, probes not needed at startup). Attempt boot with minimal configuration. Why: A failing peripheral can block or freeze the boot sequence.

steps.title: Observe Error Messages or LED Codes
steps.instructions: Document any displayed error codes or warnings. Check the system display for BIOS or software initialization messages. Why: Error codes provide guidance on the root cause.

steps.title: Soft Reset / Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Turn off the system completely. Wait 1–2 minutes, then power on. Why: Clears temporary faults that can block boot.

steps.title: Network and Software Checks
steps.instructions: Disconnect from network if errors appear during network initialization. Ensure the system is running supported software versions if a recent upgrade was performed. Why: Network or software conflicts can delay or prevent boot.

CCR.Complaint: System will not complete boot; remains on manufacturer logo.
CCR.Cause: External power verified; all peripherals disconnected; system still fails to boot.
CCR.Resolution: System removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent to Siemens Healthineers service for evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Error codes or LED behavior documented
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - Touchscreen or Control Panel Issues
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound systems with unresponsive or erratic touchscreen and control panel behavior.

steps.title: Verify Power Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the system is properly plugged into a tested, live electrical outlet. Check for loose or damaged power cords. Why: Low or intermittent power can cause control electronics to behave erratically.

steps.title: Check System Boot and Status Lights
steps.instructions: Observe startup indicators and error messages. Confirm the system completes booting without alerts related to control panel or touchscreen. Why: Some touchscreen or panel issues appear only during or after boot errors.

steps.title: Inspect Touchscreen and Control Panel Surface
steps.instructions: Clean the screen and buttons with approved non-abrasive cleaner. Remove any protective covers or films that could interfere with capacitive touch. Why: Debris or moisture can prevent proper input registration.

steps.title: Test Input with Alternate Method
steps.instructions: If available, connect a mouse or use keyboard shortcuts to navigate menus. Observe if control issues persist when bypassing touchscreen or panel. Why: Helps isolate whether the problem is the display/panel or the main system.

steps.title: Check for Loose or Damaged Cables
steps.instructions: Inspect ribbon or connection cables between the touchscreen/control panel and main system. Re-seat cables if accessible without disassembly. Why: Intermittent contact can mimic failure.

steps.title: Perform a System Restart
steps.instructions: Power down the system completely, wait 30–60 seconds, then power on. Observe if touchscreen responsiveness improves. Why: Temporary software glitches can affect input responsiveness.

steps.title: Verify Software Updates and Calibration
steps.instructions: Check if the system requires touchscreen calibration (if available in menu). Confirm system software is up to date. Why: Software inconsistencies can affect panel behavior.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen and control panel buttons intermittently unresponsive.
CCR.Cause: Observed touchscreen and panel do not register input; no debris or loose cords found; system software up to date.
CCR.Resolution: Device labeled Out of Service; sent for bench evaluation with Siemens Healthineers service.

helpfulDetails: Startup indicators and error messages noted.
Screen and panel cleaned and visually inspected.
Final device status: Out of Service pending repair.

title: Siemens Healthineers ACUSON Sequoia - DICOM/PACS Transfer Failures
description: Troubleshooting guide for ACUSON Sequoia ultrasound system DICOM/PACS transfer failures, focusing on external network and configuration checks.

steps.title: Verify Network Connectivity
steps.instructions:
Ensure the ultrasound system is properly connected to the hospital network.
Check Ethernet cable connections, Wi-Fi if applicable, and network port lights.
Why: Network connectivity is a common cause of PACS transfer failures.

steps.title: Ping the PACS Server
steps.instructions:
Use system network tools to ping the PACS server IP.
If the server is unreachable, note the network error.
Why: Confirms whether communication is blocked at the network level.

steps.title: Check DICOM Configuration
steps.instructions:
Verify the AE Title, IP address, and port of the PACS server in the Sequoia DICOM settings.
Ensure there are no typos or misconfigurations.
Why: Incorrect DICOM settings prevent proper image transfer.

steps.title: Confirm PACS Server Availability
steps.instructions:
Check if the PACS server is online and accepting connections.
Coordinate with the IT department if necessary.
Why: Even with correct settings, the PACS server must be operational.

steps.title: Test with Another Modality or Workstation
steps.instructions:
If possible, send a test image from another system to the same PACS destination.
Why: Helps isolate whether the issue is with the Sequoia or the PACS/network.

steps.title: Review System Logs
steps.instructions:
Check the Sequoia DICOM transfer logs for error codes.
Note recurring messages or patterns.
Why: Logs often indicate the source of failure (network timeout, server refusal, etc.).

steps.title: Restart DICOM Service or System
steps.instructions:
Restart the Sequoia DICOM service or perform a system reboot if external checks pass.
Why: Clears temporary software or communication issues.

CCR.Complaint: Sequoia ultrasound system cannot send images to PACS; error ‘DICOM connection failed.’
CCR.Cause: Network connectivity verified; PACS server reachable; Sequoia DICOM configuration checked—settings correct; logs show repeated timeouts.
CCR.Resolution: Escalated device to vendor for internal software/communication evaluation; system labeled Out of Service; alternative imaging modality provided for patient exams.

helpfulDetails: PACS server status at time of failure
Attempts from other devices to same PACS destination

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Sample Aspiration Failure or Clot Detection Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e blood gas analyzers experiencing sample aspiration failures or clot detection errors.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Never troubleshoot a blood gas analyzer while it is actively being used for patient testing. Ensure clinical staff have access to another functioning analyzer before beginning troubleshooting or maintenance.

steps.title: Verify the device is powered on and fully initialized
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer has completed startup and is not in standby, calibration, or maintenance mode. Sample aspiration will not occur if the instrument is performing automated processes.

steps.title: Confirm the sample type and container are correct
steps.instructions: Verify that the syringe or capillary tube used is compatible with the RAPIDPoint sampling system. Improper containers or adapters may prevent proper aspiration.

steps.title: Inspect the sample for clots or improper mixing
steps.instructions: Blood gas samples must be properly mixed after collection. Small clots or inadequate mixing can trigger clot detection or block aspiration. Gently roll the syringe if the sample appears separated.

steps.title: Check the sample probe area for visible contamination or obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the probe tip and sample port for dried blood, debris, or residue that could restrict sample uptake.

steps.title: Perform the analyzer’s automated probe cleaning function
steps.instructions: Use the system’s maintenance menu to run a probe rinse or cleaning cycle. This often clears minor buildup within the probe.

steps.title: Verify cartridge installation and status
steps.instructions: Confirm the reagent cartridge is correctly installed and not expired. Some aspiration errors occur when the cartridge fluid pathway cannot support proper sample flow.

steps.title: Check waste and fluid levels
steps.instructions: Ensure the waste container is not full and that system fluids (wash or reagent fluids) are available. Improper fluid levels can interrupt aspiration cycles.

steps.title: Run a quality control (QC) test
steps.instructions: Running QC can help determine if the aspiration pathway is functioning normally. If QC samples aspirate successfully, the issue may be related to the patient sample.

steps.title: Inspect tubing and sample pathway connections (external areas only)
steps.instructions: Without opening the analyzer, visually check accessible tubing and connections for kinks, disconnections, or leaks that may affect sample flow.

steps.title: Restart the analyzer
steps.instructions: A controlled reboot can reset fluid handling routines and clear temporary aspiration or sensor errors.

CCR.Complaint: User reported the RAPIDPoint 500 analyzer would not aspirate patient samples and repeatedly displayed clot detection errors.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found dried blood residue on the sample probe and evidence of poor sample mixing from multiple syringes.
CCR.Resolution: Performed probe cleaning cycle, cleaned accessible probe area, and verified proper sample handling with staff. Analyzer returned to normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Any alarms or error messages displayed
Final device status (returned to service or removed from service)

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Sensor Cartridge Recognition or Installation Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for situations where a Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 or 500e blood gas analyzer fails to recognize a sensor cartridge or reports installation errors.

steps.title: Confirm the analyzer is not actively processing a patient sample
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is idle before troubleshooting. Interrupting an active process may create additional system errors or corrupt cartridge data.

steps.title: Verify the correct sensor cartridge type is installed
steps.instructions: Confirm the cartridge model matches the analyzer configuration and is within its expiration date. Incorrect or expired cartridges may fail recognition during initialization.

steps.title: Check cartridge installation and seating
steps.instructions: Open the cartridge compartment and confirm the cartridge is fully seated and latched according to manufacturer design. Improper seating can prevent electrical contacts from aligning with the analyzer.

steps.title: Inspect the cartridge contacts and connector area
steps.instructions: Remove the cartridge and visually inspect the electrical contact area for dust or debris, moisture or condensation, and physical damage to contacts. If debris is present, gently clean the area using approved lint-free materials and avoid using excessive force or liquid.

steps.title: Reinstall the sensor cartridge carefully
steps.instructions: Reinsert the cartridge and ensure it locks into place securely. Watch the display for initialization messages confirming cartridge detection.

steps.title: Restart the analyzer
steps.instructions: If the cartridge is still not recognized, perform a controlled power cycle by turning the analyzer off, waiting approximately 30 seconds, and powering the analyzer back on. This allows the system to reload cartridge configuration and communication.

steps.title: Observe system messages during startup
steps.instructions: During boot-up, monitor the screen for cartridge recognition messages or system error codes. These can help determine whether the analyzer is detecting the cartridge but rejecting it.

steps.title: Check for physical damage to the cartridge
steps.instructions: If recognition errors persist, inspect the cartridge for cracked plastic housing, bent or missing contact pins, or fluid leakage. If any of these conditions are present, the cartridge should not be used.

steps.title: Try a known-good sensor cartridge
steps.instructions: If available, install another cartridge that is known to be functional and within expiration date. If the analyzer recognizes the second cartridge, the original cartridge is likely defective.

steps.title: Review system alerts or maintenance logs
steps.instructions: Check the analyzer's alert history or maintenance logs for cartridge recognition errors or related system faults that may indicate a recurring issue.

CCR.Complaint: Staff reported that the RAPIDPoint 500 analyzer displayed a 'Sensor Cartridge Not Recognized' error after replacing the cartridge.
CCR.Cause: Sensor cartridge was not fully seated in the cartridge bay, preventing proper contact with the analyzer connector.
CCR.Resolution: Removed and reinstalled the sensor cartridge, ensuring it was fully seated and latched. Analyzer successfully recognized the cartridge and returned to normal operation.

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Automatic Calibration Failures or QC Lockouts
description: Guide for troubleshooting Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e blood gas analyzers reporting automatic calibration failures or QC lockouts.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Is Not Currently in Use
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is not actively processing a patient sample. Calibration or QC troubleshooting should only be performed when the instrument is idle to avoid interrupting clinical workflows.

steps.title: Review the On-Screen Error Message
steps.instructions: Check the analyzer display for specific calibration or QC error messages. Note whether the issue involves: Calibration failure, QC failure, QC overdue or lockout, Sensor drift or instability. This information helps determine whether the issue is related to the sensor cartridge, QC material, or analyzer conditions.

steps.title: Verify Sensor Cartridge Installation and Status
steps.instructions: Check the installed sensor cartridge for proper seating in the cartridge bay, correct installation date, and expiration status. A cartridge nearing expiration or recently installed may temporarily produce calibration errors until the system stabilizes.

steps.title: Check Calibration Pack and Reagent Status
steps.instructions: Review reagent and calibration pack levels in the analyzer menu. Confirm packs are not empty or expired, reagent tubing is properly connected, and no kinks or disconnections are present. Low or expired reagents commonly cause calibration failures.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample and QC Ports
steps.instructions: Look for dried blood, debris, or residue around the sample port or QC aspiration area. If contamination is present, carefully clean the area according to hospital policy and ensure no obstruction prevents proper aspiration. Blocked ports can interfere with calibration and QC sampling.

steps.title: Verify QC Material Condition
steps.instructions: If the issue involves QC lockout, confirm the QC ampoules or cartridges are within expiration, ensure QC material has been stored according to manufacturer recommendations, and verify the correct QC level is being used. Improper QC material handling can cause repeated QC failures.

steps.title: Confirm Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Blood gas analyzers are sensitive to environmental factors. Verify that room temperature is within normal operating range, ventilation openings are not blocked, and the device is not exposed to excessive heat or cold. Temperature instability can affect sensor calibration.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer
steps.instructions: If all external checks appear normal, perform a controlled restart of the analyzer and allow the system to complete its initialization and calibration sequence. Temporary software conditions can sometimes cause calibration interruptions.

steps.title: Observe the Next Calibration Cycle
steps.instructions: After restart, monitor the next automatic calibration cycle. Confirm that calibration completes successfully and no additional QC lockout messages appear. If calibration succeeds, the issue was likely temporary or environmental.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer reports repeated automatic calibration failures and displays QC lockout message preventing patient testing.
CCR.Cause: Sensor cartridge approaching expiration and QC material found expired during inspection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced expired QC material and allowed analyzer to complete successful automatic calibration. QC run passed and analyzer returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Analyzer restarted and calibration cycle observed

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – Sample Path Blockage or Fluidics Pump Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting sample aspiration failures caused by blockages or fluidics pump issues on RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e analyzers.

steps.title: Verify Power and Warm-Up
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is fully powered on and has completed its initialization sequence. A device not properly warmed up may fail to aspirate or trigger fluidics errors.

steps.title: Check Sample and Tubing
steps.instructions: Confirm that the sample syringe, capillary, or tube is properly loaded and not expired or clotted. Inspect tubing for kinks, pinches, or bends that could obstruct flow. Replace the sample line or cartridge if there is visible contamination or blockage.

steps.title: Inspect the Fluidics Pump Area
steps.instructions: Visually check the pump mechanism for obstructions such as debris, coagulated sample, or dislodged tubing. Ensure tubing is fully seated in the pump channels.

steps.title: Run a Manual Aspirate / Flush Test
steps.instructions: Use the device’s maintenance menu to run a manual sample aspirate or flush. Observe whether the device successfully moves fluid through the system. If the sample fails to aspirate, note any specific error codes displayed.

steps.title: Check Consumables and Reagents
steps.instructions: Ensure the sensor cartridge or reagent pack is correctly installed and not empty or expired. Misalignment or low reagent levels can mimic pump failure symptoms.

steps.title: Verify Sample Path Sensors
steps.instructions: Inspect the optical or pressure sensors along the fluidics path for dirt, moisture, or misalignment. Clean sensors carefully according to external maintenance procedures—do not disassemble internal components.

steps.title: Swap Cables and Connectors (if applicable)
steps.instructions: For analyzers with removable fluidics modules, reseat connectors and ensure there are no loose connections.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer reports “Sample path blockage” during routine blood gas analysis.
CCR.Cause: Sample line observed kinked and fluidics pump could not aspirate sample; flush test failed.
CCR.Resolution: Tubing replaced, pump visually inspected, manual flush attempted; persistent failure—unit labeled Out of Service and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Sample line condition (kinked, clotted, or correct)
Final device status (Out of Service, sent for repair)

title: Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e Series – LIS / Network Communication Failures
description: Guide to troubleshoot Siemens RAPIDPoint 500 / 500e blood gas analyzers that cannot transmit results to LIS or hospital network.

steps.title: Verify Network Connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer is physically connected to the network via Ethernet or verified Wi-Fi. Check that cables are secure and undamaged. Confirm the correct port and switch connection. Why it matters: Loose or damaged network cables are a common cause of LIS communication failures.

steps.title: Check Analyzer IP Configuration
steps.instructions: Navigate to the network settings on the analyzer touchscreen. Confirm the IP address, subnet mask, and gateway match the network requirements. If using DHCP, verify the device has obtained an address. Why it matters: Incorrect network configuration prevents the analyzer from reaching the LIS.

steps.title: Ping Test
steps.instructions: Use a networked computer or the analyzer’s diagnostic menu to ping the LIS server or network gateway. Successful pings indicate network connectivity is present. No response may indicate a network issue, firewall block, or switch port problem.

steps.title: Verify LIS Server Availability
steps.instructions: Confirm the LIS server is online and accepting connections. Coordinate with IT to check if any server maintenance or network restrictions are in place. Why it matters: If the server is down, communication failures are expected and not device-related.

steps.title: Check LIS Interface Settings
steps.instructions: Ensure the analyzer’s LIS interface parameters (protocol, port, destination) match those configured in the LIS. Confirm the correct device ID or unit number is being used. Why it matters: Mismatched parameters can block data transmission even on a healthy network.

steps.title: Inspect Software and Firmware
steps.instructions: Verify that the analyzer software/firmware is up to date. Review any logs for communication errors or failed transmissions. Why it matters: Outdated software or firmware can cause intermittent LIS connectivity issues.

steps.title: Reboot Analyzer and Network Devices
steps.instructions: Power-cycle the RAPIDPoint analyzer and network switches if external checks pass. Re-test communication. Why it matters: Temporary network glitches or device errors can be resolved with a controlled reboot.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer fails to transmit results to LIS; ‘communication error’ displayed on touchscreen.
CCR.Cause: Network cable disconnected and analyzer could not ping LIS server.
CCR.Resolution: Secured Ethernet cable, verified IP configuration, rebooted analyzer. Communication successfully restored.

helpfulDetails: Network cable type and port verified
Analyzer IP address and subnet configuration
LIS interface settings (protocol, port, device ID)
